reconstruction_method: Coptic phonological survival + comparative Afroasiatic (Semitic/Berber) + Greek/Neo-Assyrian transcriptions of Egyptian proper names + internal consonantal skeleton of hieroglyphs
scholarly_consensus: Consonants relatively secure: ejectives (pʼ tʼ kʼ), pharyngeals (ꜥ/ḥ), emphatics, and glottal (ꜣ); vowels reconstructed as /a i u/ with length contrast, heavy dialectal variation; unstressed reduction to schwa-like [ə] common in Egyptological convention.
confidence_overview: medium-low overall (high–medium on consonants and ejectives/pharyngeals; low on exact vowel timbre, stress, and dialect splits)
vowel_reconstruction_note: Hieroglyphs record a consonantal skeleton only — vowels from Coptic reflexes + transcriptions/AA cognates (high uncertainty on exact quality/timbre/dialects; schwa-like unstressed common). Egyptologists insert epenthetic vowels (e, a) by convention; these are not written in originals.

vowels: /a i u/ with length /aː iː uː/ (Coptic and transcription evidence); unstressed [ə] epenthetic; exact timbre disputed across Nile Valley dialects
consonants: Stops: p t k (plain) + pʼ tʼ kʼ (ejectives); Fricatives: f s z ḫ [x] h; Pharyngeals: ꜥ [ʕ] ḥ [ħ]; Emphatics: d (cuneiform ṭ evidence); Nasals m n; Liquids r l; Glottal ꜣ [ʔ]
phoneme_inventory.ayin_aleph_distinction: ꜥ (ayin) = voiced pharyngeal fricative [ʕ]; ꜣ (aleph/glottal) = glottal stop [ʔ] or vowel onset — distinct in Coptic reflexes and Semitic loan comparisons
disputed_phonemes: exact vowel qualities in stressed vs unstressed syllables
j/y and w as consonants vs vowels
phoneme_inventory.distinctive_features: Ejectives + pharyngeals; no written vowels; Afroasiatic consonant template; Coptic provides latest direct phonological witness

ipa_reconstructed.sample_text: m rn.f n ra (formula: 'in the name of Ra')
ipa_reconstructed.confidence_note: Consonants and pharyngeal ra (r+ꜥ) secure; vowel insertions Egyptological convention; stress on content words inferred from Coptic and comparative AA
ipa_reconstructed.alternatives: Allen: [mə ɾan.nəf ən ˈɾaːʕa]; Loprieno dialect variants; vocalization of rn.f as [ɾan.nakʰ] in other contexts (suffix-dependent)

stress_rules: Stress likely on penultimate or final content syllable (Coptic and Afroasiatic comparative evidence); exact Middle Egyptian dialect stress partly unknown

prosody.rhythm: Syllable-timed with epenthetic vowels breaking consonant clusters
prosody.pacing: Formulaic religious/royal diction; deliberate pacing in ritual phrases
prosody.notes: Hieroglyphic writing omits vowels entirely — audio generation must use reconstructed vocalization tags

grok_imagine_guidance: Commit to: ejectives pʼ/tʼ/kʼ (sharp glottal pop after stop); pharyngeal ꜥ [ʕ] (deep throat constriction, Arabic ع); emphatic/velarized consonants heavier than plain; glottal stop ꜣ where marked. Approximate: epenthetic schwa [ə] in unstressed slots; pure a/i/u vowels without English diphthongs. Avoid: pronouncing hieroglyphs as consonant-only strings; English th for ḫ; treating ꜥ as silent. Flag: ꜥ and ejectives have no English equivalents — use Arabic/Ge'ez reference clips; all vowels are reconstructed [reconstructed] tags on screen.
tutoring_hooks: Hieroglyphs spell CONSONANTS only — every vowel you hear is a scholarly guess from Coptic!
Egyptian ꜥ (ayin) is a deep throat sound [ʕ] — not a vowel, not silent.
Coptic is the time machine: the last stage of Egyptian with full vowels written.
Ejectives (pʼ tʼ kʼ) pop from the glottis — rare in European languages, common in Afroasiatic.
The formula 'life, prosperity, health' (ꜥnḫ wḏꜣ snb) adorned every noble's tomb — consonants attested, vowels reconstructed.

sources.author: James P. Allen
sources.title: Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs (3rd ed.)

sources.author: Carsten Peust
sources.title: Egyptian Phonology: An Introduction to the Phonology of a Dead Language

disputed_fields: exact vowel qualities and dialect variation
